name: Route of the Menhires of Valdeolea
name_original: Ruta de los Menhires de Valdeolea – circular desde Mataporquera
description: Cycle the Route of the Menhires of Valdeolea, a moderate 13.8-mile (22.2 km) loop from Mataporquera, exploring ancient megaliths and diverse
region_intro: Touring cycling around Valdeolea offers varied landscapes, from tranquil river valleys to rolling mountains. The region, part of Cantabria's "Green Valleys," features a well-maintained road network suitable for cycling. Routes often traverse lush, verdant terrain and can include challenging climbs. The area's mild, temperate climate supports year-round cycling. Best touring cycling routes around Valdeolea The most popular touring cycling route is Mining Basins Route, a 32.2 miles (51.9 km) trail that takes 3 hours 45 minutes to complete. This route explores historical industrial landscapes. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Route of the Menhires of Valdeolea, a moderate 13.8 miles (22.2 km) path. This route allows cyclists to explore ancient megaliths. Local touring cyclists also love the Aguilar Reservoir – Collegiate Church of St. Michael loop from Cillamayor, a 31.8 miles (51.2 km) trail leading through varied terrain, often completed in about 3 hours 18 minutes. Touring cycling around Valdeolea is defined by green valleys, rolling mountains, and serene river valleys. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate loops to more challenging rides. The routes in Valdeolea are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.7 stars from more than 30 reviews. More than 500 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Valdeolea's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available around Valdeolea?
A: There are over 80 touring cycling routes around Valdeolea, offering a wide range of options for different skill levels and preferences.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on touring cycling routes in Valdeolea?
A: Valdeolea offers varied landscapes, from tranquil river valleys to rolling mountains. You can expect to cycle through lush, verdant valleys and across more challenging mountainous terrain, particularly in areas bordering the Montaña Palentina. The region is known for its well-maintained road network, providing smooth surfaces for both leisurely rides and more ambitious climbs.

Q: Are there any family-friendly touring cycling routes in Valdeolea?
A: Yes, Valdeolea offers routes suitable for families. While specific 'family-friendly' routes are not detailed, the region's varied terrain includes moderate loops that can be enjoyed by cyclists of different abilities. Look for routes with lower elevation gain and shorter distances for a more relaxed family experience.

Q: What historical or natural landmarks can I see along the touring cycling routes?
A: Many routes in Valdeolea integrate historical and natural points of interest. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1012454461/route-of-the-menhires-of-valdeolea" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Route of the Menhires of Valdeolea&lt;/a&gt; allows you to explore ancient megaliths. Other routes might pass by Roman ruins, charming villages, or offer views of the Aguilar Reservoir. You can also find nearby attractions like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2573626" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Source of the Ebro River&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3672317" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ruins of Julióbriga&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What is the best time of year for touring cycling in Valdeolea?
A: Thanks to the Gulf Stream, Valdeolea enjoys a mild, temperate climate, making it suitable for cycling throughout the year. Summers are pleasantly warm, and winters remain gentle, ensuring comfortable riding conditions. This means you can plan your touring cycling trip almost any time.

Q: Are there any circular touring cycling routes available?
A: Yes, many touring cycling routes around Valdeolea are designed as loops. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/24633807" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Aguilar Reservoir – Collegiate Church of St. Michael loop from Cillamayor&lt;/a&gt;, which offers a varied terrain experience. Another is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31879970" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alto del Bardal – Historic Bridge of Reinosilla loop from Cillamayor&lt;/a&gt;, providing a longer, more challenging ride.

Q: What do other touring cyclists enjoy most about cycling in Valdeolea?
A: The touring cycling routes in Valdeolea are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.7 stars from more than 30 reviews. Cyclists often praise the region's varied landscapes, from tranquil river valleys to rolling mountains, and the opportunity to explore historical sites like ancient megaliths and Roman ruins.

Q: Are there routes that explore historical industrial areas?
A: Yes, for those interested in historical industrial landscapes, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1170948303/mining-basins-route" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mining Basins Route&lt;/a&gt; is a popular option. This 32.2-mile (51.9 km) trail takes you through areas with a rich mining heritage.

Q: Are there any routes that pass by Roman ruins?
A: Indeed, you can explore Roman history on two wheels. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40311531" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;By the River – La Llanuca Roman Ruins loop from Los Carabeos&lt;/a&gt; is a route that specifically integrates historical sites like Roman ruins into its path, offering a blend of physical activity and historical exploration.

Q: What is the general difficulty level of touring cycling routes in Valdeolea?
A: Valdeolea offers a range of difficulties. While there are some moderate routes, the majority of touring cycling routes are considered difficult, with 62 out of 84 routes falling into this category. This indicates that many routes feature significant elevation changes and longer distances, appealing to more experienced cyclists.

Q: Are there any specific routes that are good for exploring ancient sites?
A: Absolutely. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1012454461/route-of-the-menhires-of-valdeolea" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Route of the Menhires of Valdeolea&lt;/a&gt; is an excellent choice for exploring ancient megaliths. This moderate 13.8-mile (22.2 km) path offers a unique cultural dimension to your ride.

Q: Can I find cafes or accommodation near the cycling routes in Valdeolea?
A: Cycling through Valdeolea and its surroundings often means encountering charming villages. These villages typically offer local hospitality, including cafes and restaurants where you can refuel. While specific accommodations are not listed, the presence of these villages suggests options for rest and refreshment along your journey.
tips: It weighs three tons and is 3 meters high, with 2.20 meters visible. About 5,000 years ago it was carried a distance of one km, until it was buried in the area of Los Juncales, in the town of Reinosilla (Valdeolea). It was carved out of sandstone conglomerate, a mass of compacted sand in which small encrusted pebbles can be seen. It is one of the 8 menhirs that make up the so-called Valdeolea menhirs route.

They were located in 1998 completely covered with vegetation. They are together, just 10 meters from each other and they are the smallest menhirs of all those in Valdeolea. La Matorra I is 1.60 meters high, visible, the buried part being unknown, and an estimated weight of 1.7 tons. It has two engraved incised crosses. La Matorra II has a total height of 2.40 meters of which 1.60 meters are visible and a weight of 2 tons. It has on one of its sides an area produced by the sharpening of weapons or tools.

It is in the town of La Cuadra. It has a total height of 2.35 meters, of which only 1.80 meters are visible, and an estimated weight of 1.2 tons. The largest of the fragments still had some curious engravings.

It has a total height of 3.78 meters and a weight of 4 tons. It had been knocked down and displaced from its original location, which is unknown, on a farm some 200 meters away. It was moved to its current position and hoisted inside a metallic structure that recreates its original vertical position.

It is the largest of those found in Valdeolea and one of the largest in Spain, with a total height of 4.85 meters, of which 3.85 meters are visible, and an estimated weight of 5 tons. Before its restoration it was divided into two halves. It has engravings on both sides.
It is 4 meters high, of which 2.75 meters are visible, and weighs approximately 4.3 tons.
It has a total height of 3.68 meters, of which 2.55 meters are visible, and an estimated weight of 3 tons. It has a cross engraved with semicircles in the four quarters.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The Route of the Menhires is approximately 22.2 kilometers (13.8 miles) long. For touring cyclists, it generally takes around 1 hour and 40 minutes to complete, though this can vary based on your pace and how often you stop to explore the historical sites.

faqs.question: Is this cycling route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: The route is classified as 'moderate' for touring cyclists. While it's designed to be accessible, it does involve an elevation gain of about 244 meters (800 feet) and a mix of surfaces, including potentially unpaved sections. Good physical condition is recommended, and some parts might require dismounting and pushing your bike.

faqs.question: Where does the Route of the Menhires cycling trail start?
faqs.answer: The circular route officially starts and ends in Mataporquera, a town known for its railway heritage. You can begin your journey from various points within the town.

faqs.question: Is there parking available at the trailhead in Mataporquera?
faqs.answer: Yes, Mataporquera, as the starting point for this circular route, offers parking options within the town. Specific parking areas are usually available near the town center or main access points.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the Route of the Menhires?
faqs.answer: This route is rich in historical and natural attractions. Key highlights include eight ancient menhirs, such as the Menhir of Peñahincada, La Matorra I and II Menhirs, and the impressive Menhir El Cabezudo. You'll also encounter the unique El Pinar Pintado (Painted Pine Forest), the Romanesque Church of Santa María La Real, sections of an ancient Roman road, and the historic Casasola Bridge.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle the Route of the Menhires?
faqs.answer: Spring is often recommended as the best time to cycle this route. The landscape is vibrant, sometimes likened to 'the Spanish Tuscany,' and the weather is generally pleasant for outdoor activities. Autumn can also offer beautiful scenery with changing foliage.

faqs.question: What is the historical significance of the menhirs on this route?
faqs.answer: The menhirs along this route are Neolithic megalithic stones, dating back approximately 5,000 years. They are among the largest in Spain, with some, like El Cabezudo, standing nearly 5 meters tall. These monuments were erected by early agricultural communities and are aligned in a way that suggests astronomical significance, possibly coinciding with the sun's path during the summer solstice.

faqs.question: Is the Route of the Menhires dog-friendly for cyclists?
faqs.answer: While the trail itself is generally open, specific rules for dogs, especially regarding leashes, may apply in certain areas or near historical sites. It's always best to keep your dog on a leash, especially when cycling, to ensure their safety and respect for other visitors and wildlife.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required for this route?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle the Route of the Menhires of Valdeolea. The trail and its historical sites are generally accessible to the public.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Route of the Menhires shares sections with other official trails. Notably, it covers about 54% of the Ruta de los Menhires trail, 21% of the Camino Olvidado (Bimón-Matamorisca), 15% of the Camino del Besaya: Cantabria, and 10% of the Asturian Way.

faqs.question: Are there places to eat or drink near the route?
faqs.answer: Mataporquera, the starting and ending point of the circular route, offers options for food and drink. The town is historically known for its traditional "ollas ferroviarias," so you might find local eateries to refuel after your ride.
